message: URGENT: Your Chase account has been locked. Verify now at chase-secure-login.com or lose access permanently within 12 hours.

red_flags: Fake urgency with 12-hour deadline
Suspicious domain (chase-secure-login.com is not chase.com)
Threat of permanent account loss
Generic greeting instead of your name
analysis.explanation: This is a phishing email trying to steal your Chase login credentials. The link goes to a fake website. Chase will never email you with urgent deadlines threatening to lock your account.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Delete this message
Log into chase.com directly to check your account
Call Chase at 1-800-935-9935 if concerned
analysis.safe_alternatives: Open your browser and type chase.com directly. Log in there to verify your account status.

message: Hello dear, I am Barrister Ahmed from Nigeria. My late client left $12.5 million with no next of kin. I need a foreign partner to claim the funds. You will receive 40%. Reply with your bank details.

red_flags: Classic Nigerian advance-fee scam pattern
Stranger offering millions of dollars
Asks for bank details upfront
Too good to be true offer
Claims about deceased client with no heirs
Will eventually ask you to pay various fees
analysis.explanation: This is one of the oldest scam types on the internet. There is no money, no deceased client, and no inheritance. If you respond, they will ask you to pay various fees (taxes, legal fees, transfer fees) that keep getting larger. You will never receive any money.
recommended_actions: Do NOT respond
Do NOT share any personal or banking information
Mark as spam and delete
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: No legitimate financial opportunity comes from a stranger emailing you about millions of dollars. Delete and forget.

message: Hi Mom, I lost my phone and this is my new number. Can you send me $500 through Zelle? I'm in trouble and need it right now. Please don't call my old number.

red_flags: Claims to be family member with new number
Urgently requests money via Zelle (hard to reverse)
Tells you NOT to call the real number to verify
Vague about what the trouble is
Zelle payments are instant and irreversible
analysis.explanation: This is a family impersonation scam. The person texting you is NOT your child. Scammers send this message to thousands of people hoping some will have children and panic. They specifically tell you not to call the old number because your real child would tell you it is a scam.
recommended_actions: Do NOT send money
Call your child at their known phone number immediately
If you can't reach them, contact another family member to verify
Do not continue texting with this number
Report the number to your carrier
analysis.safe_alternatives: Call your child directly at the number you have saved. If they truly lost their phone, they can contact you through other means like email or a friend's phone.

message: Your prescription from CVS Pharmacy is ready for pickup at the Main St location. Refill #RX-4471982.

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard prescription ready notification from CVS Pharmacy. It includes a specific pharmacy location and refill number, which is consistent with legitimate pharmacy notifications.
recommended_actions: If you have prescriptions at CVS, this is likely legitimate
Verify by calling your CVS pharmacy directly
Check the CVS app for your prescription status
analysis.safe_alternatives: You can always verify by opening the CVS app or calling your pharmacy location directly.

message: ALERT: Someone just used your debit card for $892.47 at Best Buy in Miami, FL. If this was NOT you, reply STOP to block your card immediately.

red_flags: Creates panic about unauthorized charges
Asks you to reply to the text (engagement trap)
Real banks don't ask you to reply STOP to block cards
Designed to get you to call back or share information
Specific dollar amount to seem more believable
analysis.explanation: This is a smishing (SMS phishing) scam. While it mentions a specific amount to seem real, your bank will never ask you to reply to a text to block your card. If you reply, they may ask for your card number, PIN, or other details to steal from you.
recommended_actions: Do NOT reply to this text
Call the number on the back of your actual debit card
Log into your bank's app to check for real transactions
Report the text to 7726 (SPAM)
Block this phone number
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are worried about unauthorized charges, call the phone number printed on the back of your physical debit card. Never use a phone number or link from a suspicious text.

message: You've been selected for a $750 Costco gift card! Take our 30-second survey to claim. Limited time offer for loyal members.

red_flags: You were not randomly selected for anything
Survey scams harvest personal information
Costco does not give away $750 gift cards via random texts
Creates urgency with 'limited time'
Will likely ask for credit card to pay for 'shipping'
analysis.explanation: This is a survey scam. The survey will collect your personal information, and at the end they will ask for a credit card number to pay for 'shipping' the gift card. There is no gift card. Costco does not run promotions like this.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click any links
Do NOT take the survey
Delete the message
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: Costco's real promotions are on their official website costco.com and in their app. They never text random people about gift card giveaways.
